title: Ensure that the --bind-address argument is set to 127.0.0.1

input_row.title: Ensure that the --bind-address argument is set to 127.0.0.1
input_row.description: Do not bind the scheduler service to non-loopback insecure addresses.
input_row.rationale: The Scheduler API service which runs on port 10251/TCP by default is used for health and metrics information and is available without authentication or encryption. As such it should only be bound to a localhost interface, to minimize the cluster's attack surface Impact: None
input_row.audit: Run the following command on the Control Plane node: ps -ef | grep kube-scheduler Verify that the --bind-address argument is set to 127.0.0.1
input_row.remediation: Edit the Scheduler pod specification file /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube- scheduler.yaml on the Control Plane node and ensure the correct value for the -- bind-address parameter Default Value: By default, the --bind-address parameter is set to 0.0.0.0 References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/command-line-tools-reference/kube- scheduler/

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**PROGRAM NAME:** k8s_scheduler_bind_address_localhost_enforced

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- Query the kube-scheduler static Pod manifest, typically located at /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-scheduler.yaml, using the Kubernetes API (via kubectl get pod -n kube-system -o yaml or by accessing the file if running as a static Pod).
- Parse the command or args section of the kube-scheduler Pod spec to identify the --bind-address flag.
- Validate that the --bind-address flag is explicitly set to 127.0.0.1.
- Pass Criteria: The kube-scheduler container command or args includes --bind-address=127.0.0.1.
- Fail Criteria: The --bind-address flag is missing, set to 0.0.0.0, or set to any value other than 127.0.0.1.
- Example kubectl command:
  ```
  kubectl -n kube-system get pod -l component=kube-scheduler -o jsonpath="{.items[*].spec.containers[*].command}"
  ```
  or, for static Pods (if not managed by kubelet as a managed Pod), inspect the manifest directly (requires host access, but CSPM tools can collect this via DaemonSets or privileged access).
- Automation can be performed by CSPM agents or Kubernetes controllers that have access to the manifest or can query the running Pod spec.

**SOURCE:** CIS KUBERNETES BENCHMARK V1.11 PDF
